articles.title: PLAID ADDS $382K IN PIPELINE WITH TLDR CAMPAIGN
articles.summary: Plaid achieved their best-performing direct buy to date, adding $382k in pipeline, earning 70 leads, and achieving 20.1x ROI. This case study breaks down their strategy, results, and example ads. Read the

articles.title: LLMS VS. MARKETPLACES
articles.summary: Google has long tried to take profit away from lead gen marketplaces. OpenAI is now going for that pool with ChatGPT. Its technology makes search much better by understanding intent, and it can transact on users' behalf, allowing the company to push further into the marketplace stack than Google and do everything a transactional marketplace can. Many marketplaces will come out of this transition just fine.

articles.title: CONTEXT IS THE NEW MOAT
articles.summary: Different teams can use the same model but get different results. The context is now the moat. Context is the structured knowledge that helps models understand specific situations. Good context results in better outputs, which can, in turn, improve context for future runs.

articles.title: GROWTH IS NOW A TRUST PROBLEM
articles.summary: Traditional distribution channels are falling apart. SEO is getting killed by AI slop and people going straight to ChatGPT for answers. Paid search costs are skyrocketing. Corporate social gets no juice from the algorithm. What's working now is employee-led social, creator partnerships, and community-driven growth. They all feed into word of mouth. On the retention side, AI is commoditizing the value props most SaaS companies were built on. If your pitch is "faster than the manual approach," you're now competing with something that costs $20/month. When capabilities get commoditized, users stick with products they trust.

articles.title: 28 HARD-WON STARTUP LESSONS AFTER 20+ FAILED ATTEMPTS
articles.summary: The author distills lessons from two decades of failed startups, emphasizing validating demand early, prioritizing users over ego, and building before chasing investors or co-founders. He argues for bootstrapping, pricing higher, avoiding free tiers, hiring only doers, and shipping fast with minimal teams and boilerplates. He also warns against hype cycles, consumer apps, corporates, and long-running dead projects, while highlighting SEO, daily writing, and audience alignment as long-term advantages.

articles.title: COWORK
articles.summary: Cowork lets Claude work as a task-based digital coworker by accessing local files to read, edit, and execute work end-to-end with user oversight.

articles.title: ATOMS
articles.summary: Atoms is an AI agent platform that researches, designs, builds, and deploys full-stack applications with integrated business functionality.

articles.title: ALPINE
articles.summary: Alpine is an all-in-one team workspace that combines documents, tasks, chat, and AI agents with shared context across work.

articles.title: WHAT DOES IT TAKE TO IPO?
articles.summary: Back in 2021, every founder said they were two years away from an IPO. Just a couple actually made it. Only 5% of unicorns are both above $300M ARR and have a Rule of 40+ score. The other 95% are zombies. Of the 52 cloud companies that have IPOed since 2018, only 7 have beaten the QQQ ETF. If you actually are on the path, IPO planning should start 18-24 months out. Accounting is the number one reason for delayed IPOs, and the list of what finance teams need to get right is longer than most founders expect.

articles.title: THE PHYSICAL AI DEPLOYMENT GAP
articles.summary: Physical AI could be the biggest market in human history, but most robotics companies are stuck demoing instead of deploying. A system that works 95% of the time in the lab might drop to 60% in the real world. Production requires 99.9% reliability. At 95%, a picking robot fails 50 times per day, and each failure needs a human to step in. The gap won't close through research breakthroughs alone but through infrastructure and tooling that don't exist yet. This article maps exactly where those opportunities are for founders building in the space.

articles.title: TO THOSE WHO FIRED OR DIDN'T HIRE TECH WRITERS BECAUSE OF AI
articles.summary: Companies relying on AI instead of human tech writers risk producing inadequate documentation, lacking empathy and context.

articles.title: COST OF BUILDING AN MVP (WITH AI)
articles.summary: Building the MVP for Lazyweb.com cost $19,100, with the domain alone taking up $13,000 due to its descriptive SEO advantages.

articles.summary: Gathering valuable advice requires intentional effort and a strategic approach.
